coredata.dc:description: In this paper, an S-type NCN/CdS heterojunction was formed by anchoring cadmium sulfide on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-modified g-C3N4 (abbreviated as NCN) via hydrothermal and calcination methods. The bifunctional properties of NCN/CdS for the detection and degradation of antibiotics [tetracycline (TC) and chlortetracycline (CTC)] were demonstrated. The S-type heterojunction has a strong redox capacity and effectively separates photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, which improves photocatalytic performance. NCDs is located between cadmium sulfide and g-C3N4, which forms a charge-transport channel that accelerates the carrier transport between the interfaces of the S-type heterojunction. Good photocatalytic performance (TC: 98.0 % for 45 min; CTC: 100 % for 70 min) and excellent cycling stability were obtained. This study provides a new strategy for designing efficient, stable and bifunctional materials for environmental pollution treatment.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-modified graphitic carbon nitride/cadmium sulfide nanomaterials for antibiotic selective detection and photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan, 750021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, an S-type NCN/CdS heterojunction was formed by anchoring cadmium sulfide on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-modified g-C3N4 (abbreviated as NCN) via hydrothermal and calcination methods. The bifunctional properties of NCN/CdS for the detection and degradation of antibiotics [tetracycline (TC) and chlortetracycline (CTC)] were demonstrated. The S-type heterojunction has a strong redox capacity and effectively separates photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, which improves photocatalytic performance. NCDs is located between cadmium sulfide and g-C3N4, which forms a charge-transport channel that accelerates the carrier transport between the interfaces of the S-type heterojunction. Good photocatalytic performance (TC: 98.0 % for 45 min; CTC: 100 % for 70 min) and excellent cycling stability were obtained. This study provides a new strategy for designing efficient, stable and bifunctional materials for environmental pollution treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of novel rapid detection and degradation methods has attracted much attention to solving the residue safety problems of antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC) and chlortetracycline (CTC) due to their widespread use. Traditional methods for detecting antibiotic residues in food or water include high-performance liquid chromatography [1], immunological methods [2], microbiology [3], and mass spectrometry [4]. All these methods have the disadvantages of time-consuming, high cost, and inconvenience. And fluorescence detection method has become the focus of attention due to its high sensitivity, good selectivity, and convenience [5,6]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a satisfactory candidate for fluorescence detection due to its superior optical properties, biocompatibility, ease of synthesis, fast response, and robust thermal/chemical stability [7–9]. However, the initially obtained g-C3N4 exhibits a bulk structure with low fluorescence quantum efficiency (∼5 %) and poor adsorption capacity, so the sensing ability is insufficient. Therefore, the energy bands of g-C3N4 can be tuned by doping and constructing composites to improve its fluorescence sensitivity [5,10–13]. The internal filtering effect (IFE) of g-C3N4-based fluorescent probes is an effective strategy in fluorescence spectral analysis. In contrast to other sensing mechanisms, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), IFE does not require intermolecular interactions between the fluorophore and the absorber [10,14], which provides a simple alternative for designing g-C3N4-based fluorescent sensors.

In addition, a range of biodegradation, physical, and chemical methods have been reported for the removal of TC and CTC, among others, in the environment [15–19]. Among them, g-C3N4-based nanomaterials have shown good structural stability and reusability in degrading TC and CTC [20,21], which is one of the research directions of wide interest in recent years. However, g-C3N4 photocatalytic activity is limited in practical applications by the high recombination rate of its photogenerated carriers. Besides, cadmium sulfide is of interest because of its narrow bandgap. but its high photogenerated carrier complexation and self-oxidation rates lead to low photocatalytic activity and severe photo corrosion, respectively [22]. To overcome the high photogenerated carrier complexation rate of the above two materials at the same time, constructing heterojunctions is a good strategy [23]. Cadmium sulfide is one of the most suitable materials to form heterojunctions with g-C3N4 [24,25]. Li et al. introduced CdS into g-C3N4 and synthesized S-type CdS/g-C3N4 composites with good photocatalytic activity for H2 generation [26]. Compared with the traditional type II heterojunction, the S-type heterojunction retains and enhances the redox capabilities of the two semiconductors, and the built-in electric field and energy band bending promotes charge transfer at the heterojunction interface, which contributes to the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency [27–29]. Furthermore, anchoring the S-type heterojunction as a channel can further accelerate the carrier migration between the heterojunction interfaces [30,31]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) not only act as charge transport channels, but also enhance the utilization of light, contributing to improved photocatalytic performance [32]. Furthermore, the introduction of N atoms modulates the charge distribution of nearby C atoms for better electron transfer and light absorption [28,33]. Wang et al. synthesized S-type SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction composites using a two-step method, and the construction of S-type heterojunctions promoted carrier separation while retaining the strong reduced electrons in CNNT. In addition, the NPCDs form a unique charge transport channel that accelerates carrier transport between the S-type heterojunction interfaces. It gives the material a high photocatalytic hydrogen production activity [30]. Fluorescence selective detection and photocatalytic degradation of TC and CTC by g-C3N4-based nanomaterials are currently rare. Based on this, our work explores the fluorescence selective detection and photocatalytic degradation properties of nitrogen-doped quantum dot-modified g-C3N4/cadmium sulfide nanomaterials for TC and CTC in aqueous solution.

4.66 mmol Cd(NO3)2 • 4H2O and 11.95 mmol thiourea were added to 50 mL of ethylenediamine, Stirred for 30 min, transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene bottle and reacted at 160 °C for 24 h. It was cooled and filtered, washed with H2O and EtOH, and dried to give a yellow powder (Fig. S1).

According to our previous studies, NCDs were synthesized by microwave irradiation from citric acid and urea in a 1:1 mass ratio. The g-C3N4 sample was obtained by heating 5 g of melamine to 550 °C, holding for 4 h, and cooling naturally. NCDs/g-C3N4 (NCN) (grey) was synthesized by a hydrothermal method [34,35].

3 mg of CdS was taken in 40 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. Then 300 mg of g-C3N4 was added, stirred for 9 h and filtered and dried. Finally, it was placed in a muffle furnace at 180 °C for 5 h, and obtained a light yellow CdS/g-C3N4 sample after natural cooling.

Based on the above study, 3 mg of CdS was dispersed into 40 ml of deionized water, and then 300 mg of NCN was added, stirred for 9 h, then filtered and dried, and finally put into a 180 °C muffle furnace for 5 h, and then cooled naturally to obtain NCN/CdS (light grey).

NCN/CdS was dispersed in deionized water and sonicated for about 45 min to obtain a suspension of 1 mg/mL, which was then left overnight and set aside. 50 μL of 10.0 mM aqueous antibiotic solution was added to 1.95 mL of the above suspension. We used several common antibiotics such as levofloxacin (LOFL), ofloxacin (OFL), neomycin sulfate (NS), streptomycin sulfate (STS), chloramphenicol (CM), sulfamethoxymethane (SMM), sulfamethoxydimethoxymethane (SDM), gatifloxacin (GAT), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), tebuconazole (TMP), chlortetracycline (CTC) and tetracycline (TC) as the substance to be measured (Fig. S2), and related fluorescence sensing studies were carried out.

Adsorption equilibrium was reached by adding 40 mg of NCN/CdS to 50 mL of 20 mg/L TC and CTC solution with no light source turned on (dark reaction) for 40 min. The light source [300 W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm)] was turned on, samples were taken at 5-min (TC) or 10-min (CTC) intervals, filtered through a microporous filter (0.22 μm), and measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Similarly, we performed blank experiments under the same conditions as described above without the photocatalyst.

The g-C3N4 shows different diffraction peaks at 13.1⁰ (100) and 27.6⁰ (002) (Fig. 1 a) [10]. Compared to g-C3N4, the NCN (002) diffraction peak shifted from 27.6⁰ to 27.67⁰ (Fig. 1b), which was attributed to the enhanced interaction between g-C3N4 and NCDs as well as the reduced layer spacing [36]. However, the diffraction peaks associated with NCDs were not found in the XRD patterns, probably due to the low content of NCDs. Characteristic diffraction peaks induced by CdS were observed at 26.7⁰, 36.8⁰, 43.9⁰, 47.7⁰, and 51.7⁰ (Fig. 1b–c), in addition to those of g-C3N4 in NCN/CdS, which corresponded to the (002), (102), (110), (103), and (112) lattice planes, respectively [37]. The intensity of the g-C3N4 diffraction peak decreases slightly after the addition of CdS, signifying that CdS has been deposited on the surface of g-C3N4. Moreover, the (002) peak of NCN is slightly shifted from 27.67⁰ to 27.74⁰, which implies that the layer spacing is reduced by CdS addition [38]. This indicates that there is a close contact between CdS and NCN. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) maps showed that NCN/CdS well preserved the main structure of g-C3N4 (Fig. 1d). The vibrational peak at 1638 cm−1 increases after the addition of NCDs, which is due to the conjugate vibration between g-C3N4 and NCDs, and the peak is also related to the CC stretching mode and the stretching mode contributes to the photoluminescence of NCDs based on the π-π* transitions [39]. However, no characteristic peaks caused by CdS were observed in NCN/CdS, which can be attributed to the low CdS content.

SEM and TEM were used to illustrate the effect of NCDs and CdS doping on the structural tuning of NCN/CdS. g-C3N4 reveals a layer structure with irregular dimensions (Fig. 2 a). CdS shows a rod-like structure of regular size (Fig. 2b). When NCDs were introduced in g-C3N4, NCN exhibited coarser and richer voids (Fig. 2c). When CdS was doped in NCN, the particles on the NCN/CdS surface were more obvious (Fig. 2d). This may be since CdS dispersed to the surface or interlayer of g-C3N4 during the sonication and stirring process. NCDs and CdS were successfully loaded onto g-C3N4 (Fig. 2e). TEM images from CdS and NCDs (Fig. S3) showed that the average particle size of NCDs was 1.45 nm. the average particle size of prepared CdS was 13.3 nm. After analysis, two stripes with different lattice spacing (NCDs: d = 0.216 nm, CdS: d = 0.329 nm) were found (Fig. 2f–g) [37,38]. The morphology and structure of NCN/CdS (Fig. 2e–g), show tightly bound interfaces, which may facilitate electron migration. Besides, the elemental mapping images (Fig. 2h-l) indicate that NCDs and CdS have been successfully introduced into NCN/CdS.

All three materials show IV-type isothermal curves with H3-type hysteresis loops and no obvious saturated adsorption plateau (Fig. S4), suggesting that these materials have an irregular pore structure, which is consistent with the observation of the SEM images. By comparing the data (Table S1), it can be found that the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of pure g-C3N4 is 16.416 m2/g, and that of NCN doped with NCDs has a BET specific surface area of 52.724 m2/g, which is 3.2 times higher than that of g-C3N4. This suggests that during the hydrothermal synthesis of the material, the NCDs not only adhered to the g-C3N4 surface, but also inserted into the material layer to a certain extent, thus increasing the specific surface area [40]. When CdS was introduced, the BET specific surface area of NCN/CdS was 53.535 m2/g, the average pore size was 9.598 nm, and the pore volume was 0.128 cm3/g. This implies that NCN/CdS has a rougher surface and more pore structures, which can provide more active sites for the catalytic reaction [41].

The surface composition and chemical structure of NCN/CdS were analyzed by XPS. The peak centers of the fitted C 1s spectra (Fig. 3 a) appeared at 284.80 eV (C–C (sp2)), 285.49 eV (CC), and 288.22 eV (N–CN) [37,42]. Compared with CdS/g-C3N4, the CC peak of NCN/CdS is shifted to a lower binding energy by 0.28 eV, which may be due to the increase in the local electron density of g-C3N4 after the introduction of NCDs [37]. The peak centers of the fitted N 1s spectra (Fig. 3b) appeared at 398.52 eV [pyridine nitrogen (CN–C)], 399.85 eV [graphitic nitrogen (N- (C)3)], 401.08 eV [pyrroline nitrogen (C–NH–C)], and 404.77 eV (charging effects.), respectively [39,43]. The significantly higher content of pyrrole nitrogen in NCN compared to g-C3N4 may be attributed to the large number of C2–NH groups in NCDs [35]. And the peaks of N 1s and C 1s in NCN/CdS are shifted to lower binding energies than NCN, suggesting that there is an interaction between cadmium sulphide and NCN, and electrons tend to be transferred from NCN to cadmium sulphide [30]. The peak centers of the fitted O 1s spectra (Fig. S5) appear at 531.33 eV (C–O) and 532.96 eV (CO) [44]. 404.82 eV and 411.59 eV were identified as Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 3c) [37,39]. and the peak centers of the fitted S 2p spectrograms (Fig. 3d) appeared at 161.28 eV, 162.49 eV, 163.53 eV, 164.70 eV, and 168.61 eV, respectively. 161.28 eV and 162.49 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [45]. Thus, NCN/CdS contains CdS, while the Cd and S elements are mainly Cd2+ and S2− respectively. 163.53 eV and 164.70 eV can be attributed to S–C and S–N bonds [46–48]. The position of the C 1s and N 1s peak in NCN/CdS is shifted toward a lower binding energy, also indicating the presence of S–C and S–N bonds [46]. 168.51 eV is believed to be some sulfate produced during calcination, which can also be verified by the shift of the characteristic peaks of O 1s to lower binding energies (Fig. S5) [49]. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, Cd 3d and S 2p of NCN/CdS were shifted to lower binding energies compared with those of pure CdS, NCN, CdS/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4, which indicated that there was a strong electronic interaction between NCN and cadmium sulphide. Moreover, XPS patterns obtained after xenon irradiation at room temperature show (Fig. S6) that the N–CN, N-(C)3 and C–NH–C binding energies of NCN/CdS are shifted to the lower energy levels, while the Cd 3d and S 2p binding energies are shifted to the higher energy levels. This may be due to the light-induced electron migration from CdS to NCN [25]. The XPS analyses further confirmed the successful preparation and formation of tightly bonded interfaces of NCN/CdS, which was favourable for the electron migration to form an internal electric field.

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 4 a) show that the absorption edges of g-C3N4 and CdS are about 460 nm and 562 nm, respectively, which are attributed to the intrinsic band gaps of g-C3N4 and CdS [50]. In addition, CdS contributes to the broadening of the absorption edge of NCN/CdS (478 nm), which extends the photoresponse range. This may be attributed to the upconversion property of NCDs and the effective combination of CdS and NCN [51]. The corresponding band gap values (Eg) of CdS, g-C3N4, NCN, and NCN/CdS were 2.36, 2.77, 2.75, and 2.73 eV (Fig. 4b).

PL measurements were performed to explore the photoinduced charges separation efficiency in the catalysts, which is beneficial in explaining the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the NCN/CdS. As shown in Fig. 4c, Compared with g-C3N4, NCN exhibits a lower PL intensity and an obvious blue shift, which can be attributed to the effective extension of the lifetime of photogenerated carriers by doped NCDs, alteration of the charge transfer rate [52] and quantum confinement effect due to the change in the density of electronic states [53,54], respectively. The fluorescence emission intensity of NCN/CdS is lower than that of g-C3N4 and NCN, which can be attributed to the inhibition of photogenerated e−-h+ pair recombination by CdS doping [55]. In addition, the PL emission intensity of NCN/CdS is lower than that of CdS/g-C3N4, suggesting that the introduction of NCDs improves the charge separation, and NCDs can act as an electronic mediator to guide the electron transfer at the NCN/CdS interface [51]. Therefore, the significant quenching of fluorescence emission implies that the heterojunction constructed in NCN/CdS effectively prevents the light-induced charge recombination, whereas the NCDs acting as electronic mediators effectively facilitate the charge transfer between g-C3N4 and CdS. Fig. 4d shows the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra of g-C3N4, NCN and NCN/CdS. The average fluorescence lifetime values of g-C3N4, NCN and NCN/CdS were 1.1260, 0.9419 and 0.8786 ns, respectively, as fitted by the double-parameter exponential equation (Table S2). The shorter fluorescence lifetimes of NCN and NCN/CdS exhibited faster photoexcitation transfer, which was attributed to the formation of heterojunctions; at the same time, the fluorescence lifetimes of NCN/CdS were shorter than those of NCN, which suggests the facilitating effect of the heterojunction after the introduction of CdS. It is further confirmed that the heterojunction structure effectively accelerates the charge separation and transfer process [28]. The transient photocurrent response provided another conclusive evidence to demonstrate the charge migration behaviour of NCN/CdS (Fig. 4e.) The results showed that all samples displayed fast and reproducible photocurrent responses at each exposure. It can be seen that the highest photocurrent intensity was observed for NCN/CdS, which indicates that the photogenerated carrier lifetime of NCN/CdS was effectively prolonged due to the construction of the heterojunction and modification of the NCDs, and the electron-hole pair separation was significantly enhanced, which is consistent with the PL analysis. The EIS Nyquist plot demonstrates the interfacial charge transfer behavior of NCN/CdS (Fig. 4f). By using the ZSimpWin software to fit the data we measured and obtained the typical Randle circuit model for g-C3N4, NCN, CdS/g-C3N4, and NCN/CdS. It can be clearly found that in the high-frequency region, the order of the diameters is as follows: NCN/CdS < NCN < CdS/g-C3N4 < g-C3N4, which suggests that the resistance to photocurrent carrier transfer is the lowest at the NCN/CdS interface, and that the photogenerated charge separation is excellent, which is in agreement with the interpretation of the PL spectra. The results support the construction of heterojunctions where the introduction of NCDs accelerates the interfacial electron transfer, enhances the e−-h+ pair separation, and reduces the photocurrent carrier transfer resistance.

By observing the NCN/CdS PL spectra (Fig. S7), NCN/CdS1% was the optimal modification amount and was applied to the fluorescence sensing experiments of antibiotics. The luminescence intensity of NCN/CdS changed to different degrees after the addition of different equal concentrations of antibiotic solutions to the 1.95 mL suspension (Fig. 5 a). In all added antibiotics, TC and CTC significantly quenching the fluorescence emission of NCN/CdS, implying that NCN/CdS can act as a fluorescent sensor for TC and CTC. To further demonstrate the quantitative fluorescence sensing ability of NCN/CdS for TC and CTC, we performed fluorescence titration experiments and quantitatively described the sensing effect using the Stern-Volmer (S–V) formula [6]. The emission intensity of NCN/CdS decreased steadily with increasing TC or CTC concentration (Fig. 5b and d), and the S–V curves showed good linearity in the low concentration range (0–0.100 mM). The values of Ksv and correlation coefficient R2 for TC and CTC can be obtained by fitting as TC: 4.74 × 103 M−1, 0.9991; CTC: 6.38 × 103 M−1, 0.9989, respectively (Fig. 5c and e). and the larger value of Ksv for CTC compared to TC implies that the NCN/CdS sensing effect on CTC is better than that of TC. The detection limits (DL) [56]: TC: 3.24 μM, CTC: 2.39 μM. In addition, by comparing with other known fluorescent materials for TC and CTC detection (Table S3), NCN/CdS has an advantage in terms of sensitivity and detection limit.

Immunity and cyclability are two important factors for evaluating the potential of fluorescent sensors for applications. Therefore, we performed competition and cycling experiments of NCN/CdS on TC and CTC. The results of the competition experiments showed that even with the addition of interferences, the detection of TC and CTC by NCN/CdS, respectively, could still maintain a high resolution (Figs. S8a–b). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of NCN/CdS (Figs. S8c–d) remained almost constant after five sensing cycles. In addition, since the FT-IR and PXRD of NCN/CdS did not change significantly from the original samples after 5 uses (Fig. S9), this suggests that NCN/CdS has very good chemical stability. To sum up, these results indicate that NCN/CdS has a certain application value in the field of antibiotic detection.

We will try to elucidate the possible sensing mechanisms of NCN/CdS on TC and CTC as follows: First, considering that there was little effective overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TC and CTC and the fluorescence emission spectra of NCN/CdS (Fig. S10), the FRET mechanism was ruled out [57,58]. However, the fluorescence excitation spectra of NCN/CdS partially overlapped with the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TC and CTC, showing the possibility of IFE. Secondly, the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore remains constant if IFE occurs, whereas the fluorophore lifetime decreases due to the energy transfer during FRET. Thus the fluorescence lifetime can be used to distinguish between the two quenching mechanisms described above. The transient fluorescence spectra indicated that the fluorescence lifetime of NCN/CdS changed from 0.879 ns to 1.084 ns and 0.943 ns after interaction with TC and CTC, respectively, with a negligible change in fluorescence lifetime, which indicated that the sensing by TC and CTC was a static process (Fig. S11) [59,60]. This further verifies that the mechanism of TC- and CTC-induced fluorescence bursting is IFE rather than FRET.

NCN/CdS efficiently degraded TC and CTC under visible light (Fig. S12). It can be observed that TC and CTC are relatively stable and difficult to degrade without a photocatalyst (Fig. 6 a–b). g-C3N4 (TC: 63.9 % and CTC: 78.4 %), CdS (TC: 78.2 % and CTC: 71.9 %) and CdS/g-C3N4 (TC: 80.2 % and CTC: 78.2 %) both showed low activity for catalytic degradation after light exposure. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared NCN was enhanced (TC: 85.8 % and CTC: 93.3 %). The photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of TC and CTC reached 98.0 % and 100 %, respectively, after further doping with CdS. To understand the reactive kinetic behavior of NCN/CdS for pollutant degradation, TC and CTC degradation data were further fitted by quasi-first-order kinetics. It can be seen that the quasi-first-order correlations fitted to the experimental data are good [57]. Calculations showed that the reaction rates of NCN/CdS for photocatalytic degradation of TC and CTC were 3.58 and 2.92 times higher than those of g-C3N4 and 2.27 and 3.64 times higher than those of CdS, respectively. In summary, NCN/CdS can effectively degrade TC and CTC under visible light.

The reusability of NCN/CdS was investigated by cyclic photocatalytic degradation of TC and CTC under visible light irradiation. In the degradation experiments of TC and CTC, NCN/CdS could achieve 96.37 % and 98.90 % degradation rates after four cycles, respectively (Fig. 6c–d). This indicates that NCN/CdS has high stability in practical applications. Since the catalysts after four uses did not show significant changes in FT-IR and PXRD for the unused former samples compared to the pristine samples (Fig. S13), this can further indicate that NCN/CdS has good chemical stability. And the thermogravimetric profiles of NCN/CdS materials before and after the use are not significantly different (Fig. S13), which can fully demonstrate that the overall structure of the prepared materials is very stable. By comparing with other known materials for TC and CTC degradation (Table S4), NCN/CdS has stable properties and superior TC and CTC degradation. This implies that NCN/CdS has a greater potential for development in the field of photocatalytic organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible intermediates of TC and CTC and their degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: The intermediates of TC and CTC degradation by NCN/CdS were resolved using LC-MS to infer the degradation pathways of TC and CTC [61–63]. The possible degradation pathways of TC (Fig. 7 ). The peaks of these intermediates are shown in Fig. S14. Firstly, TC reacts via ring opening to form P1 (m/z = 467). P1 reacts via demethylation, oxidation, and dehydrogenation to form P2 (m/z = 437). P2 is oxidized to form P3 (m/z = 191). Moreover, TC demethylates to form P4 (m/z = 415), and P4 deaminates and ketones to give P5 (m/z = 397) and P6 (m/z = 360). the cyclic hydrocarbon structure of P6 was disrupted to form P7 (m/z = 318) and P15 (m/z = 338). P7 was demethylated to form P8 (m/z = 274). further oxidative decomposition and ring opening of P8 resulted in the formation of P9 (m/z = 228), P10 (m/z = 149) and P11 (m/z = 127). As well, P7 was dehydrated and decarbonized to form P12 (m/z = 301). P12 forms P13 (m/z = 282) and P14 (m/z = 249) by ring opening and dehydrogenation. These intermediates are eventually broken down into harmless molecules such as CO2 and H2O by the strong oxidizing action of free radicals.

Intermediates with different m/z ratios were detected in the CTC degradation (Fig. S15). Based on the m/z assay products and previous studies [63], two possible CTC degradation pathways (Path 1 and Path 2) were proposed (Fig. S16). First, The CTC molecule (m/z = 515) loses HCl to form P1 (m/z = 478). Second, the degradation of P1 follows two pathways. In path 1, P1 loses chloride ions to form P2 (m/z = 444), which is further deamidated and dehydrated to form P3 (m/z = 385), and the loss of -N-(CH3)2 in P3 forms P4 (m/z = 338), P5 (m/z = 282) was obtained after the breakage of the P4 carbocycle. P5 obtained P6 (m/z = 195) and P7 (m/z = 181) by decarboxylation and ring-opening reactions induced by double-bond breakage; In path 2, P1 was dechlorinated to give the intermediate P8 (m/z = 444), P8 was deamidated and dehydrated to give P9 (m/z = 361), P10 (m/z = 274) was formed by loss of -N-(CH3)2 and –CH3 in P9, further decarboxylation and carbocyclic ring breakage resulted in the formation of P11 (m/z = 136), and P11 was formed by decarboxylation and carbocyclic ring breakage to form P12 (m/z = 92), P13 (m/z = 102) and P14 (m/z = 74). These intermediates are further oxidized and eventually turned into CO2, H2O, and inorganic ions such as Cl− and NO3 −.

To investigate the mechanism of NCN/CdS degradation of TC and CTC, free radical capture experiments and EPR tests were performed. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as capture agents for holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), respectively. Fig. 8 a–b shows the effect of different capture agents on the degradation rates of TC (45 min) and CTC (70 min). we confirmed that the degree of contribution of active species in catalytic process: h+ > •O2 − > •OH. Therefore, EPR was used to detect h+, •O2 −, and •OH with DMPO and TEMPO as capture agents. Under dark conditions, the •O2 − and •OH signals have no background peaks, but the h+ does. If the material produces h+ in the presence of light, the capture agent binds to the h+, and the signal is weakened [64]. Fig. 8c shows a significant change in the peak signal of h+ after light exposure, which indicates that NCN/CdS produces h+ under light conditions. When BQ was added to remove h+, the photocatalytic TC and CTC degradation rates decreased from 98 % to 100 %–21.4 % and 50.6 %, respectively. Fig. 8d shows that under light conditions, the •O2 − content of the solution increases with the duration of light exposure. After illumination for 7 min, the typical quadruple peaks of DMPO - •OH adducts were detected (Fig. 8e), while the intensity of the peaks was weak.

Based on the analyses of PL, photocurrent curves and electrochemical impedance maps, In order to further explore the charge transfer mechanism during the photocatalytic process, the EPR technique can be implemented with DMPO as the capture agent to generate DMPO - •OH and DMPO - •O2 − to evaluate •OH and •O2 − production, respectively [51,65]. Pure g-C3N4 does not exhibit the characteristic signal of DMPO - •OH because the valence band potential is not suitable for the generation of the hydroxyl group (Fig. S17). The strong signal of DMPO - •OH in NCN/CdS indicates that the photo-induced h+ of CdS on VB is not transferred. This phenomenon is not consistent with the traditional type II heterojunction charge transfer mode. In addition, the DMPO - •O2 − signal of NCN/CdS is strong, indicating that the photogenerated e− remains on the CB of g-C3N4 and does not migrate to the CB of CdS. The apparent enhancement of the EPR signals of DMPO - •OH and DMPO - •O2 − of NCN/CdS can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the introduction of NCDs and heterojunction formation [51].

Based on this, The Eg of CdS and g-C3N4 are 2.36 eV and 2.77 eV, respectively. the ECB and EVB of the prepared materials were determined using VB-XPS and Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) [66]. The work functions of CdS, NCDs, and g-C3N4 are 5.22, 3.18, and 3.36 eV, respectively (Fig. 9 a). The formula is as follows [37]: Φ = hν − Ecutoff. The energy gaps from the Fermi energy level to the VB maximum are 0.86 and 1.80 eV for CdS and g-C3N4, respectively (Fig. 9b–c). Therefore, the energy levels (Δ) of VBM in g-C3N4 and CdS were calculated to be 5.16 eV and 6.08 eV, respectively. In addition, the CB was calculated as ECB = EVB - Eg, resulting in a CB of 3.70 eV and 2.39 eV for CdS and g-C3N4, respectively. Finally, normalized by the reversible hydrogen electrode potential (RHE, 0 V vs. −4.44 eV) [67], CdS (EVB: 1.64 eV, ECB: −0.74 eV), g-C3N4 (EVB: 0.72 eV, ECB: −2.05 eV).

Thus, a possible NCN/CdS energy band structure is proposed. CdS and g-C3N4 have a staggered energy band structure (Fig. 9d). Upon contact, the electrons at the interface move from g-C3N4 and NCDs (higher Fermi energy levels) to CdS (lower Fermi energy levels) until the Fermi energy levels reach equilibrium. In this process, NCDs act as charge transfer channels located between CdS and g-C3N4 facilitating rapid electron transfer. The electrons on the g-C3N4 side of the interface are depleted while the electrons on the CdS side are accumulated, forming an in-built electric field (IEF) along the CdS to g-C3N4 (Fig. 9e), which produces energy band bending [30,68]. Hence, under the influence of IEF, energy band bending and Coulombic interactions under light, e− in CdS CB is more readily complexed with h+ in g-C3N4 VB via a charge transport channel (NCDs) to form S-type heterojunctions (Fig. 9f) [69]. The photogenerated e−-h+ pairs in CdS and g-C3N4 were efficiently separated, resulting in the retention of more reduced e− in the CB of CdS and more oxidized h+ in the VB of g-C3N4, which enhanced the photocatalytic ability of NCN/CdS [70].

Therefore, we can reasonably propose a possible reaction mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of TC and CTC by NCN/CdS. EVB (+1.62 eV and +0.72 eV) is lower than •OH/H2O (+2.8 eV), while ECB (−0.74 eV and −2.05 eV) is lower than O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV). Thus, NCN/CdS directly produces •O2 − during catalysis. Moreover, •O2 − can be converted to H2O2 (0.895 eV) [71]. The photocatalytic mechanism of TC and CTC degradation by NCN/CdS is shown in Fig. 10 . For TC and CTC photodegradation, large amounts of separated e− and h+ were produced by NCN/CdS under visible light irradiation. h+ can directly participate in the oxidative decomposition process of TC and CTC, while e− can reduce dissolved O2 to form •O2 −, which then oxidatively decomposes TC and CTC.

In summary, NCN/CdS constitutes an S-type heterojunction, which enhances the electronic excitation and slows down the photogenerated e−-h+ complexation to achieve the efficient degradation of TC and CTC.

In this paper, we prepared S-type NCN/CdS heterojunction nanomaterials by hydrothermal and calcination methods and used them for the selective detection and photocatalytic degradation of TC and CTC in aqueous solution. Fluorescence sensing experiments showed that NCN/CdS could detect TC and CTC via fluorescence quenching with high sensitivity and very good selectivity, and the corresponding detection limits were 3.24 μM and 2.39 μM, respectively. Competition and sensing cycling experiments showed that NCN/CdS has good resistance and cyclability to TC and CTC. Transient fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy indicated that the mechanism of NCN/CdS detection of TC and CTC could be the internal filter effect (IFE). The S-type heterojunction has a strong redox capacity and effectively separates photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, which improves photocatalytic performance. The NCDs are located between cadmium sulfide and g-C3N4, forming a charge transport channel that accelerates carrier transport between the S-type heterojunction interfaces. Therefore, the reaction rates of NCN/CdS for photocatalytic degradation of TC and CTC were 3.58 and 2.92 times higher than those of g-C3N4 and 2.27 and 3.64 times higher than those of CdS, respectively. The degradation of TC and CTC by NCN/CdS was higher than 96 % after 4 cycles of use. The intermediates produced during the degradation process were also detected by LC-MS, The possible TC and CTC degradation pathways were investigated. Free radical capture experiments and EPR tests confirmed that •O2 − and h+ play a major role in the photocatalytic degradation process. All these indicate that NCN/CdS is a promising material for TC and CTC treatment that combines detection and photodegradation.
